t: AI-Driven Observability for Trustworthy Agentic AI
e: Agents return a clean 200 OK and still be wrong, unsafe, or expensive. Why agentic AI needs a new observability layer — LLM-as-judge, safety metrics, and the four lifecycle stages.

t: Governance with the Agent Governance Toolkit
e: OWASP Agentic Top 10 coverage with YAML policy files, two API surfaces, and a metric bridge that shows policy denials in Grafana.

t: Refactor: from rolled-our-own to MAF-native
e: I built memory, communication, security, governance, and evals from scratch first. Then I deleted most of it and used the MAF-native packages. Here is the audit table and what survived.

t: Multi-turn evals from first principles
e: Single-turn evals check one decision. Multi-turn evals check the whole trajectory. A Python harness with three evaluators, an offline test suite, and the judge prompt that actually works.

t: Observability for multi-agent: traces and metrics
e: OpenTelemetry through MAF's configure_otel_providers, custom workflow spans, custom metrics for runs/duration/agent selection, Jaeger + Prometheus + Grafana wiring, and the set-once latch gotcha.

t: A2A: when the workflow IS the broker
e: The reference architecture distinguishes request-based and message-driven agent communication. For in-process orchestration, the workflow IS the broker — and A2A is just the wire format.

t: Memory done right with MAF
e: AgentSession is short-term memory. MemoryContextProvider + MemoryFileStore is long-term memory. Mem0 is long-term memory for serious workloads. The boundary that matters and how to implement each.

t: Agent registry as a project-local convention
e: The Microsoft Agent Framework deliberately does not ship an agent registry. Here is why that is the right call, and what to build as a project-local convention when you need one.

t: Ollama as the default LLM for enterprise-shaped systems
e: PROVIDER=ollama, granite4.1:3b, zero API keys, no Azure account. How to make a multi-agent project that demonstrates enterprise patterns without requiring enterprise infrastructure.

t: Five MAF orchestration shapes — adding Group Chat and Magentic
e: The first ten posts treated MAF as having four orchestration patterns. The official docs say five. Here are the two I missed — Group Chat and Magentic — and why they matter.

t: Four orchestration patterns in MAF — and when to pick each
e: Sequential, Concurrent, Handoff, and Custom WorkflowBuilder. Four shapes the Microsoft Agent Framework ships out of the box, when to pick each, and the gotchas that cost me a day.

t: The 12-chapter reference architecture, in 102 Python files
e: Microsoft published a 12-chapter reference architecture for multi-agent systems and a separate framework (MAF) to build them. Here is what the 102 Python files actually contain and how they map to the chapters.

t: Lessons from Converting 18 Agents in 90 Days
e: What worked, what was hard, and what we'd do differently. Real numbers: 18 agents, 90 days, 5 governance policies, 4 provider swaps.

t: Deployment & A2A: From ADK's Web Deploy to MAF's Scalable Architecture
e: Cloud Run deployments, agent-to-agent communication, load balancing, and production observability.

t: Zero Trust Isn't Optional Anymore: Securing AI Agents
e: Agents can be manipulated into misusing permissions they legitimately hold — nothing 'unauthorized' ever hits your logs. A tiered Zero Trust framework for AI agents, with the test that reframes every control: impossible vs. tedious.

t: Finding Vulnerabilities Is No Longer the Hard Part
e: AI can now find vulnerabilities faster than the world can fix them. A six-step find-and-fix loop for using LLMs to secure source code — and why the verification bottleneck is the real problem.

t: Callbacks and Middleware: Porting ADK Hooks to MAF's Decorator Pattern
e: Callbacks to middleware: composable decorators for audit, retry, token enforcement, and OpenTelemetry integration.

t: Provider Abstraction: From Gemini-Only to Swappable LLMs
e: Zero-code provider swaps: Ollama (dev), OpenAI (staging), Azure Foundry (prod). Same agents, different models.

t: Tool Wrapping: From ADK Functions to MAF Governed Tools
e: From ADK functions to MAF governed tools. Adding policy enforcement, DLP, approval gates, and OPA integration.

t: Token Exchange Patterns: Porting Multi-Turn State from ADK to MAF
e: Sessions to threads: porting multi-turn state from ADK to MAF. Token budgeting, long-term memory, and conversation audit trails.

t: The Executor Pattern: ADK→MAF Conversion for Agentic Control Flow
e: How to port ADK's orchestration callbacks to MAF builders without losing control. The executor pattern: you own the loop.

t: Why We Migrated from Google ADK to Microsoft MARA
e: The philosophy, trade-offs, and what we learned converting 18+ agents in 3 months. Provider abstraction as the foundation for portable agents.

t: OpenTelemetry and Evaluation in Multi-Agent Workflows — the full production stack
e: How to instrument multi-agent systems with OpenTelemetry, propagate trace context across an in-memory bus, and build a layered evaluation pipeline — from real-time policy gates to async LLM-as-judge to SLO-based trust scoring. Everything I learned building Genie.

t: Running a HIPAA-Aware Multi-Agent Medical AI on GKE: A Field Map
e: Google's GKE AI infrastructure docs list ~40 integrations. Here's a field map of which ones actually matter when the workload is a HIPAA-aware multi-agent medical AI, and where the gaps sit.

t: The Right-to-Explanation Handler: GDPR Article 22 as a Go HTTP Endpoint
e: How a 200-line Go handler turns an audit log and an eval store into a regulator-friendly answer to 'why did the AI decide that?'

t: Time-bound privileged access — building the PCSE §1.4 PAM analog in Go
e: Request → N-eyes approve → window-of-time → automatic expiry, with every transition written to a hash-chained audit log. The package that closes Gap #1 from the PCSE map.

t: HL7 v2 in 2026: Why This 50-Year-Old Protocol Still Drives Healthcare Integration
e: Why HL7 v2 — a 50-year-old pipe-delimited protocol — still drives most US hospital ADT integrations in 2026, and what a clean Go parser looks like in ~300 lines.

t: Studying for the AIGP? Here's a Reference Implementation in Go
e: Studying for the IAPP AI Governance Professional credential? Here's an open-source Go codebase that demonstrates ~70% of the body of knowledge in working code.

t: "Enough to Reconstruct, Never Enough to Leak": The HIPAA Audit Log Design Problem
e: Enough to reconstruct, never enough to leak. The audit event schema problem under §164.312(b), and how to solve it without conflating the audit sink with the PHI sink.

t: The Fallback Is the Contract: Reliability Patterns for Clinical AI
e: Every LLM-backed agent in this platform has a deterministic rule-based fallback. The case always finalises. The fallback isn't a workaround — it's the contract.

t: Multi-Agent Systems in 5 Interfaces: A MARA Implementation Field Report
e: Five interfaces hold the whole platform together. The 30-line orchestrator closure that makes the rest of the architecture testable, auditable, and safe to evolve.

t: PostgreSQL Row-Level Security Is HIPAA Defense in Depth
e: PostgreSQL row-level security as HIPAA defence in depth. Why fail-open application filtering isn't enough, and how 'append-only at DB GRANTs' carries more of the §164.312(b) burden than people realise.

t: The 21st Century Cures Act, Expressed in Go
e: The 21st Century Cures Act §3060 CDS carve-out criterion 4 expressed as a code-level queue, lossless on reject, with audit-recorded reviewer rationale. Build it once, satisfy GDPR Article 22 for free.

t: Moving Diagnostic Accuracy 42.9% → 85.7% by Changing Two Files
e: How a single sprint of specialty-rule work — guided by a benchmark that wasn't afraid to print embarrassing numbers — turned a 'demo respiratory differential' into a five-condition rule-based diagnostic engine.

t: Building a HIPAA-Aware Medical AI Platform in Go: An Architecture & Compliance Deep Dive
e: What HIPAA looks like when you express it as Go interfaces — governance policies, append-only audit at DB GRANTs, PHI redaction at the logger seam, and HITL as the §3060 CDS carve-out criterion 4.

t: The MERGE pattern that cost ten times more than INSERT-then-UPDATE — a ₹100 Cr lesson
e: What looked like an idiomatic BigQuery MERGE was scanning the full target table on every batch. The fix was syntactic, not architectural — and it was the single biggest contributor to a 57% data-warehouse cost reduction across the Tata Group engagement.

t: How I Cut a Fortune 500 BigQuery Bill by 57%
e: Architecture decisions that delivered 57% cost reduction on a Fortune 500 BigQuery data warehouse. The MERGE anti-pattern fix, partition strategy, capacity model, and the trust gap I'd handle differently next time.

t: Optimus — a Gemini-powered BigQuery anti-pattern detector that paid for itself in a week
e: We built a small Go + Python service that parses a project's INFORMATION_SCHEMA, asks Gemini to classify each top-spending query against a catalog of anti-patterns, and recommends a rewrite. It is not a magic box; it is a pipeline that cuts the human review time per query from 20 minutes to 90 seconds.

t: BigQuery slot reservation transitions — when to commit, when to stay on-demand
e: Capacity-based slot reservation is the biggest single FinOps lever for predictable batch workloads, but the transition is harder than the math. Notes from sizing reservations across enterprise GCP customers.

t: BigQuery storage tiering — physical bytes, logical bytes, and the savings hiding in your old partitions
e: Storage was the second-biggest line item on the Tata BigQuery bill. Long-term storage, physical-vs-logical billing, and column-level retention together took a 6-figure monthly line down to a 5-figure one.

t: The Spanner Migration Tool — a contributor's reading map
e: Notes from contributing to Google's open-source Spanner Migration Tool (HarbourBridge). Where to start reading the codebase, where the load-bearing logic lives, and the parts that look simple but aren't.

t: Primary-key design for Cloud Spanner — preventing write hotspots, 40-60% performance gains
e: Spanner partitions by primary-key range. A monotonically-increasing PK like a timestamp or UUID-v1 funnels all writes to one server. The fix changes everything from your sequence strategy to your tenant model.

t: Spanner interleaved tables — when and when not
e: Interleaving a child table into its parent co-locates the rows for fast joins. It also tightens coupling in ways that bite you on the next schema migration. A practitioner's decision matrix.

t: CDC for minimal-downtime Spanner migration — Datastream + Pub/Sub + Dataflow
e: A bulk migration takes hours; the application can't be offline that long. CDC keeps the source and destination in sync while the bulk runs, and a quick cutover swaps traffic. The handoff between bulk and CDC is where most migrations go wrong.

t: Bloom — Terraform for regulated bank cloud provisioning, SOC 2 and ISO 27001 from day one
e: Notes from contributing to Bloom — SC Ventures / Standard Chartered's policy-driven secure cloud provisioning platform. Push-to-deploy self-service for bank engineering teams, with the audit controls baked in.

t: SOC 2 controls as Terraform modules — turning the audit from a project into a daily build
e: If you encode each SOC 2 control as a Terraform module, the audit becomes a check against module usage rather than a per-resource review. Notes from Bloom and adjacent projects.

t: airshipit + OpenTelemetry — 30% reduction in manual ops in a multi-vendor OSS project
e: Notes from integrating OpenTelemetry into airshipit, an open-source bare-metal Kubernetes lifecycle project with contributions from Ericsson, AT&T, Microsoft, and others. The hard part wasn't OTel; it was making distributed traces useful across foreign code.

t: azure-service-operator — collaborating with Ericsson, AT&T, and Microsoft on a Kubernetes-native Azure controller
e: The azure-service-operator project lets you declare Azure resources as Kubernetes objects. Notes from the multi-vendor collaboration shape: how decisions got made, what slowed us down, what shipped despite it.

t: Picnic — cutting API latency 47% by consolidating microservices behind protobuf contracts
e: The Picnic social platform served 1M+ users across a graph of Go microservices behind a GraphQL gateway. The latency win came from a counter-intuitive move: fewer services, tighter contracts.

t: Picnic — 80%+ test coverage and Prometheus observability turned days-to-detect into minutes-to-detect
e: Test coverage and observability are the boring infrastructure that makes the interesting changes safe. Notes on how the Picnic team built both, and the on-call experience they enabled.

t: Globe — running a 30K+ TPS transaction platform on Kubernetes
e: The transaction engine had to absorb 30K+ TPS across partner integrations, never lose a transaction, and survive partial failures. The architecture: Go, Kafka, Pub/Sub, Redis, K8s, with idempotency at every layer.

t: Idempotency at three layers — the pattern that kept the Globe transaction engine honest
e: A single layer of idempotency will eventually fail. Three independent layers gives you a margin. Here is the pattern that worked across ingest, worker, and emit boundaries.

t: Error-code orchestration — replacing a giant switch statement with a typed enum across partner adapters
e: Status-code-based dispatch made every worker grow a longer and longer switch. Normalising every partner-specific error into an enumerated set let the orchestration logic stop changing as new partners landed.

t: Double-entry ledger invariants — building a P2P lending platform that never loses money
e: 5K+ loans per month. Three credit bureaus. Multiple payment gateways. The thing that has to be right is the ledger. Notes on what invariants the database enforces vs what the application enforces.

t: P2P lending — KYC/AML, three credit bureaus, and the maker-checker RBAC governance that kept fraud rates low
e: Borrower onboarding is the most fraud-prone moment in a P2P platform. The shape that worked: deterministic KYC, parallel bureau pulls with fallback, real-time fraud signals, and a maker-checker approval for every disbursement.

t: Brownlow — zero-trust voting on Cloud Run during live AFL broadcasts
e: 100K+ votes, 10K+ concurrent users during a live AFL Brownlow Medal broadcast. The architecture: Go on Cloud Run, GraphQL + gRPC behind a CDN, vote integrity through Cloud KMS + Security Command Center. Notes on what makes a live-broadcast load shape unusual.

t: Speaking at Google Cloud Next 2022 — the monolith-to-microservices talk
e: 30 minutes on stage. The talk title looked tactical; the talk underneath was about why most microservices migrations fail and how to set up the one that doesn't.

t: Google Summer of Code mentor 2019-2026 — what I learned guiding 10+ students through open-source projects
e: Seven cycles. Ten-plus students. Most shipped, a few didn't, all of them taught me something about engineering culture. Notes on what works for mentors and what works for students.

t: Gocloud — designing a unified API library for AWS, GCP, and Azure
e: What it actually takes to build a unified cloud API library — and why "write once, run anywhere" still doesn't quite work, even for the patterns where it almost does.

t: Mapping a multi-agent platform to the GCP PCSE blueprint
e: Every Professional Cloud Security Engineer exam bullet, mapped to a file path in an RBI FREE-AI aligned Go platform. Where the implementation matches, where the analog substitutes, and where the honest gaps are.

t: Defence in depth for agentic AI — the eleven-layer envelope
e: The mental model that says no two adjacent layers share a single point of failure for the same class of attack. From TLS to OTel, the eleven layers a customer request crosses before an answer comes back.

t: Consolidated security deep-dive — RBAC, RLS, token exchange, encryption, audit
e: The long-form security narrative for a multi-agent financial assistant — authentication, authorisation, tenant isolation, dual-identity audit, envelope encryption, hash-chained logs, governance, red team, BCP.

t: AI governance — from credential to codebase
e: Board policy as a YAML file the risk team owns. Annexure VI as a database query. Every governance recommendation rendered as a file path in a Go repository.

t: Agentic security in production — the operations playbook
e: Twelve months of running multi-agent AI in a regulated context. SLIs that matter, the incident runbook, drift detection, continuous adversarial testing, secret rotation, compliance posture as code.

t: Annexure VI as a query
e: The RBI FREE-AI incident reporting form, expressed as a Go struct and a Postgres table. Every entry is an auto-generated artefact from the runtime — not a form an operator fills in retrospectively.

t: RBI FREE-AI implementation notes — 26 recommendations to file paths
e: Every one of the 26 RBI FREE-AI recommendations, mapped to a specific file in a working multi-agent platform. What's ✅ done, what's 🟡 partial, what's ⚪ honest gap.

t: Why Go for production agentic AI
e: Stdlib over libraries, single binary over framework, fail-closed defaults over forgiveness. The boring-on-purpose case for choosing Go to ship a multi-agent system into a regulated environment.

t: BCP for AI — forced-failure drills
e: Fallback agents plus a CI step that replaces the primary agent with one that always errors. If the fallback doesn't produce a usable answer, the PR can't merge.

t: Sovereign AI is a policy, not a slide
e: Classification → provider allowlist. A pii-classified message can only reach a provider whose region is in the allowlist for pii. Sovereignty as a runtime gate, not a checkbox.

t: NPCI rail routing with human-in-the-loop
e: UPI, IMPS, NEFT, RTGS — which rail to use depends on amount, urgency, window, success-rate history. A deterministic chooser with a HITL gate above ₹2 lakh.

t: Policy as code, without the risk team having to ship code
e: A tiny CEL-style DSL plus a board-approved YAML file. The risk team adds a governance rule by editing a config file; engineering ships the rule by restarting the service.

t: Deterministic KYC, the LLM just talks
e: PAN check-digit validation, Aadhaar offline KYC, DigiLocker, PEP/sanctions — all in Go code, not in a prompt. The LLM's job is to translate the verdict into something a human can read.

t: Production agentic AI on Kubernetes — Ch 9 patterns vs reality
e: Field notes from running multi-agent AI on K8s. The patterns the book recommends, the ones that survived contact with production, and the ones that broke in interesting ways.

t: Agentic architecture on MARA — the seven load-bearing pieces
e: Microsoft's Multi-Agent Reference Architecture in Go. Protocol, registry, bus, governance, orchestration, observability, evaluation — and how the seven hold each other up.

t: JWT in 150 lines of Go — the case against the library
e: HS256 JWT issue + verify + audience check + expiry in pure stdlib. Why pulling a third-party JWT library is the wrong call for security-critical code.

t: HS256 vs RS256 — pick the wrong one and explain why
e: Symmetric vs asymmetric JWT signing. The choice changes what fails when a key leaks, who can verify, and how rotation works.

t: OAuth 2.1 + PKCE for a single-page app
e: PKCE is the load-bearing mitigation against authorization-code interception. The Go implementation is short; the parts every SPA gets wrong are documented here.

t: OAuth Device Flow (RFC 8628) — for voice assistants, kiosks, smart TVs
e: The flow where the device has no browser. User authenticates on their phone; the device polls until they're done. Implementation patterns in Go from the Genie reference.

t: WebAuthn passkeys in Go with crypto/ed25519
e: Passkeys are FIDO2; FIDO2 is the spec; Ed25519 is the signature algorithm. The full registration + assertion flow in 200 lines of stdlib Go.

t: RFC 8693 token exchange — the nurse Alice scenario
e: Dual-identity tokens for the agent → MCP server → upstream API chain. Subject stays the user; Actor identifies the agent acting on the user's behalf. Walked through with a worked clinical example.

t: SAML 2.0 verifier in Go — XML signing without losing your mind
e: Many banks have a SAML IdP they want you to federate against. The verify path is the boring-but-load-bearing piece. Notes on the stdlib-mostly Go implementation.

t: SPIFFE/SPIRE basics — workload identity at deploy time
e: Services need identity too, not just users. SPIFFE issues SVIDs (verifiable identity documents) to workloads; SPIRE is the reference issuer. The shape and the first deploy.

t: mTLS at the proxy — Envoy + SPIRE-issued SVIDs
e: Pushing mTLS into a service mesh removes it from every individual service. Envoy + SPIRE is the canonical pattern; the implementation has fewer moving parts than the architecture diagrams suggest.

t: Session anomaly detection — Haversine distance + credential-stuffing density
e: Two signals do most of the work for detecting compromised sessions: impossible travel between consecutive logins, and credential-stuffing density across an IP range. The Go implementation.

t: GraphRAG — when a knowledge graph beats vector search
e: Vector search treats every chunk as independent. GraphRAG models the relationships between entities, communities, and concepts. For corpus-spanning questions ("what's the relationship between X and Y"), graph wins.

t: BigQuery Knowledge Graph for entity resolution at scale
e: BigQuery has had a built-in knowledge graph since 2024. For entity resolution across millions of rows — the "is this John Smith the same as that John Smith" problem — it's the cheapest tool I've found.

t: HyDE — generate a hypothetical answer to improve retrieval
e: Embedding a question and embedding an answer often produce different vectors. HyDE generates a hypothetical answer to the question, embeds *that*, and retrieves on it. Retrieval quality goes up disproportionately.

t: Self-RAG and CRAG — when to retrieve, when to skip, when to correct
e: Naive RAG retrieves on every query. Self-RAG decides whether to retrieve. CRAG decides whether the retrieved content is good enough or needs corrective retrieval. Two papers; both worth implementing.

t: Multilingual RAG for India — Bhashini hooks and cross-lingual retrieval
e: An Indian banking deployment needs to handle Hindi, Marathi, Tamil, Bengali, and English in the same retrieval pipeline. Bhashini (the government's language stack) plus cross-lingual embeddings make it tractable.

t: Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol — the spec and the Go client
e: Anthropic's A2A spec standardises how agents talk to other agents (not just tools). The Go client is small; the conceptual shift is what matters.

t: Google Cloud's 12 agent design patterns — mapped to real agent implementations
e: Google publishes a 12-pattern taxonomy for agent design. Most of them have direct corollaries in production code; one or two are best ignored. The mapping I've used.

t: Saga rollback when half the steps succeeded — the unhappy path that matters most
e: A saga is fine when every step succeeds. The interesting code is what runs when step 3 of 5 fails and you have to undo 1 and 2 in the right order. The patterns I use.

t: Cost-aware agent dispatch — when the cheap agent is enough
e: Not every query needs the production agent. A cost-aware dispatcher decides whether to route to the cheap-and-fast agent or the expensive-and-thorough one. Same UX, dramatically lower bill.

t: Latency-aware agent dispatch — picking by SLO, not by capability
e: Two agents can do the same job. One takes 200ms; the other takes 5 seconds. Pick by user-facing SLO, not by which agent is "better." The dispatcher pattern.

t: The case for boring stack choices in regulated AI
e: Postgres over the latest vector DB. Go stdlib over the framework du jour. Single binary over Kubernetes operator. The choices that bore reviewers and delight on-call engineers.

t: Default-to-Prototype as a culture, not just a flag
e: An agent that doesn't declare a tier defaults to Prototype, not Production. The flag is the code; the culture is what enforces "new code is not production until someone says so."

t: embed.FS as a deployment unit — config, prompts, UI assets
e: Go's embed.FS bundles files into the binary at compile time. The pattern collapses what would be a multi-artefact deploy into one binary. Three places it pays back daily.

t: GOMEMLIMIT and the soft GC pacing change every Go service should set
e: GOMEMLIMIT tells the Go runtime to keep memory below a soft cap by running GC harder when it's close. For containers with hard memory limits, this prevents OOM kills. The setting every Go service in K8s should have.

t: slog migration — replacing five logging libraries with stdlib
e: Go 1.21 added structured logging to the stdlib (slog). For a codebase with three or four logging-library generations layered on top of each other, the migration is a productive afternoon.

t: Running AWS Bedrock and Vertex AI in the same agent stack
e: An enterprise customer wants you on AWS; the next one wants you on GCP. The provider router pattern that keeps the agent code identical and swaps only the LLM endpoint.

t: Egress costs — the gotcha that kills cloud-arbitrage plans
e: Cross-cloud data movement is billed by the GB. The bill is invisible until it isn't. A multi-region or multi-cloud architecture that doesn't model egress costs in design will discover them in production.

t: Data residency in the Gulf — UAE ADGM/DIFC + Saudi SAMA at Bancnet
e: An open-banking platform serving UAE and Saudi customers had to honour three overlapping regulators: ADGM (Abu Dhabi), DIFC (Dubai), and SAMA (Saudi central bank). Notes on the architecture that satisfied all three.

t: GKE for stateful AI workloads — the patterns that survived production
e: Multi-agent stacks have state: vector indexes, chat histories, agent memory. GKE for AI workloads needs StatefulSets, PVCs, gateway controllers, and the patterns that work in 2026.

t: Workload Identity Federation Azure → GCP for a real migration
e: Moving a workload from Azure to GCP while it continues to authenticate against on-prem Azure AD (Entra ID). Federation lets the GCP workload assume a GCP service account based on its Azure identity.

t: UPI integration — the spec quirks no one mentions
e: UPI is the most popular payment rail in India. The spec is precise. The implementation guides are not. Notes on the integration details that ate weeks the first time.

t: KYC under the RBI Master Direction vs Aadhaar offline KYC — the practical differences
e: Two KYC pathways an Indian fintech has to support. The Master Direction (Video KYC, etc.) and Aadhaar Offline KYC. Different speeds, different evidence requirements, different audit shapes.

t: Voice AI for two-wheelers — multi-language patterns from the Kinetic India work
e: A rider asks the bike a question in Marathi, Hindi, or English. The voice stack has to do ASR, intent classification, dispatch to a service tool, generate a response, TTS — all under 3 seconds. Notes from the proof-of-concept.

t: Brownlow — Cloud KMS + Security Command Center for vote integrity
e: Vote integrity needed two things the platform team couldn't fake even by accident: signing keys we couldn't access, and continuous security monitoring we couldn't silence. KMS + SCC delivered both.

t: AIGP body of knowledge — a Go engineer's reading map
e: IAPP's AI Governance Professional certification covers a body of knowledge worth knowing whether you certify or not. The mapping from BOK to working Go code for the engineer who wants to understand AI governance practically.

t: The board policy is not a slide — it's a YAML file
e: The bank's board approves an AI policy. The policy exists as a slide deck nobody reads. The risk team's actual operational policy is what's in the code. Closing that gap is the FREE-AI Rec 14 win.

t: Audit logs are the API of record
e: The audit log isn't a side effect of the system. It's the contract you owe to regulators, customers, and your future self. Treat it as a first-class API — schema, versioning, and SLOs included.

t: Twelve Go idioms I changed my mind about
e: Patterns I confidently recommended five years ago that I'd argue against today. The list of "things you used to do in Go that don't pay back anymore."

t: iter.Seq — the pull iterator pattern in Go 1.23+
e: Range-over-function landed in Go 1.23. `iter.Seq` lets you write iterators that compose. The patterns that pay back; the ones that don't.

t: errgroup patterns for parallel agent dispatch
e: Fan out to N agents; first error cancels the rest; collect successful results. errgroup is the right tool for this; the patterns are concise but worth getting exactly right.

t: Twelve months of writing in public — what worked, what didn't, what I'd cut
e: Reflections on a year of consistent technical writing. The post categories that compounded; the ones that didn't; what I'd tell someone starting out.

t: The recruiter test — what your repo says before the interview
e: A recruiter spends 90 seconds on your GitHub before deciding to talk to you. What they're looking for; what makes them skip; what signals matter more than the README.

t: Twelve months of Genie in production — what survived, what we rewrote, what we deleted
e: An honest retrospective on the open-source Genie project after a year. The patterns that held up; the ones we rebuilt; the code we deleted because it solved problems we didn't actually have.
